lessonTitle: Building a Personal University Funding Plan
concept: A funding plan is not a wish list — it is a specific, sourced, numbered account of how every year of your degree will be paid for.

hook.fact: Marcus has a university place. He has a vague sense his parents will help and he might get a scholarship.
hook.question: That is not a plan. That is a hope.

conceptCards.title: Card 1
conceptCards.body: A **funding plan** is a document — however simple — that lists: your annual TCA, every confirmed source of funding, the gap between them, and specifically how that gap will be closed. Every item should be a confirmed number, not

conceptCards.title: Card 2
conceptCards.body: The four main funding sources to combine are: family contribution, scholarships and grants, student loans, and earned income. A strong plan does not rely entirely on any single one. Diversification in funding is as important as diversification in a financial

conceptCards.title: Card 3
conceptCards.body: Confirmed vs unconfirmed: family support is unconfirmed until a specific amount and payment schedule has been agreed. A scholarship application is unconfirmed until the award letter arrives. Build your plan on confirmed sources first, then layer in likely but unconfirmed

conceptCards.title: Card 4
conceptCards.body: Contingency planning means having a written answer to: "What do I do if family support drops by 50%? What if I miss my scholarship? What if my part-time job disappears?" A plan without contingencies is a plan that collapses at

conceptCards.title: Card 5
conceptCards.body: Now test this: Your funding plan should be rebuilt annually. Year 2 is different from Year 1: startup costs disappear, scholarship applications can be renewed or new ones sought, earned income typically increases as you find better campus roles. Review

questions.prompt: What makes a source of funding "confirmed" versus "unconfirmed"?
options: It is from a government rather than a family
A specific amount and payment schedule has been agreed or awarded in
It is a loan rather than a gift
It has been received in your bank account
questions.explanation: "Confirmed means a specific number is committed — a signed scholarship award, a loan approval, or a written family agreement with a stated amount."

questions.statement: A funding plan only needs to be built once, before you start university.
questions.whyPrompt: Why is that true or false?
questions.explanation: "Circumstances change every year — scholarships expire, family situations shift, work income varies. A good funding plan is rebuilt annually."

questions.prompt: Put these funding plan steps in the correct order:
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.prompt: Match each funding source type to its reliability characteristic:

pairs.right: Lower reliability — no written amount or schedule
Medium reliability — income depends on finding and keeping the

questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: "A plan that lists each confirmed funding source, the gap between them and TCA, and a specific strategy to close that gap is called a ___." ___
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: Priya's TCA: €26,000. Confirmed funding: €15,000 family + €4,000 scholarship + €5,000 loan = €___.
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.prompt: Kai's Year 1 funding sources: family €18,000 | scholarship €3,000 | loan €5,000 | part-time work €4,500 | Total TCA: €28,000
questions.explanation: "€18,000 + €3,000 + €5,000 + €4,500 = €30,500... wait: let's recalculate. €18,000 + €3,000 = €21,000 + €5,000 = €26,000 + €4,500 = €30,500. TCA is €28,000. Surplus of €2,500 — Kai can set this aside as contingency or reduce his loan."

questions.scenario: Marcus's funding plan: "My parents will cover everything. If that doesn't work out I'll think of something."
options: He should have a student loan
He has no confirmed amounts, no contingency plan, and no fallback —
He should apply for more scholarships
questions.explanation: "A plan requires specific numbers and contingencies. 'My parents will cover it' with no written amount, no schedule, and no backup is the most common financial failure mode for international students."

tasks.scenario: Build Sofia's complete Year 1 funding plan for NUS Singapore:
tasks.prompt: Contingency question: If the tuition grant falls through, what is her new gap?
tasks.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

inputFields.label: Part-time campus work (15 hrs/wk, 30 weeks, S$14/hr)

tasks.scenario: Priya's Year 2 situation changes: her scholarship is not renewed (−€4,000) and her family increases support by €2,000.
tasks.prompt: What is the smartest way to close it?
tasks.explanation: "Break-even is a fragile position — one unexpected expense wipes it out. Priya should treat the €4,000 scholarship loss as an immediate action item and find a replacement."

lockIn.takeaway: A real funding plan has specific confirmed numbers, a named strategy for every gap, and written answers to "what if"

reviewQueueEntry.definition: Funding plan anchors better education money decisions.
reviewQueueEntry.quickRecallPrompt: What does Funding plan include beyond tuition?
